speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, and I want to know about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: You see? These I used to too.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape four with Boris Abel. I'm Sandy Jacobson, August 15, 1995. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] So what happened to you in the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: So in the United States, I come over, you know. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I couldn't speak English one word. So I was in connection-- I come over in Salem, when I find out in Lynn they got a community-- Jewish Community Center. So I took a bus from Salem to Lynn. And to me, it was harder to go from Salem to Lynn, like from Bremerhaven to United States was easier. When I come over there, and they hold me  up for a few hours. And a lady come out, and she started to talk to me like you talk to me now. All these story I had to tell her. And I say now I look something how to survive, to make a living. Because I didn't know-- nobody helped me. Now, when somebody comes in, they got support. But in this time, nobody-- I come over, one survivor in the North Shore, nobody asked me if I need something to eat. She listened to everything, and she say, OK, in not more a couple days, we call you. You got to-- we'll have a job for you. And I'm waiting still now. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was in in-- I meet a man, he got a drugstore, you know? So he talked to a man who got a toy shop. And they put me to work for $23 a week, $0.65 an hour.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I was in, I didn't have a place to go, no nothing. So I used to go in and they had a synagogue in [INAUDIBLE], and I saw the [BACKGROUND NOISE] rabbi there. There used to be a Rabbi [NON-ENGLISH]. And I talked to him. He told me, in this time a man had a gas station, when he retired, he became-- [BACKGROUND NOISE] he had a position there. So the rabbi asked him, and he was an older man, in a big house. He said, maybe you will take in this refugee. He said, yes. He took me in. I used to pay him $18 a week. And he used to give me one meal night, at night, when I used to come from work. And I worked in this shop. So I worked there for three months, and at Christmas they close up. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: When he saw I lost his-- my job, the big one, he said, my son from Philadelphia, he's a big engineer. He's coming in my room. [LAUGHS] And this is a story like bologna, you know? [LAUGHS] So I was in a hotel, I think. And I was living there for a short while. [BACKGROUND NOISE] And I meet-- this time, I meet my wife. After this, I met my wife, when I was in-- How did you meet your wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was in this drugs man. He had a child, and she brought a present there. So I meet her, you know. And she used to speak Yiddish, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you learning English in the meantime?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- when I come over, I couldn't speak one word. But after three or four months, I started to speak a little bit. And after this, I was in-- in business, to make the brushes you know. So I wasn't successful either, because I didn't know. But I find out where to buy the stuff. I buy the wrong stuff, you know. And I suffered a lot, you know. I took a place in the city Beverly for $8 a month rent. I used to freeze to death there, and I couldn't cover even $8 a month income. But I suffered, suffered, and I build up a nice successful, you know. I used to make brushes for the big factories, you know, industrial. I used to design, design brushes for them. Even now sometime I got an order sometime. They got Raytheon, a company, where they make the missiles, what they, what they used in Israel and I guess Iraq, you know. Sometimes they need a brush like this, I made up sometime. But now they took away the order, and they give it to a Texan company. Texas.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you still working?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I, I gave up a month ago. And I repaired electric razors for 47 years, 45 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you have-- tell me about your children.

speaker: interviewee
text: My children, I got my son.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Charles Abel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he graduate high school, and he graduated Brandeis College and Suffolk College. And he was a teacher in Ipswich High School for math. And one time he waked up-- no, he didn't wake up. He come over in my store and he said, I'm going to California. He left for California. Now he's 15 years in California.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you have another child?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I got another child, Susan. She's older than he. And she was a nurse.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So we're going to stop the tape, and we're going to meet daughter-- your son and your wife.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Boris, will you introduce us to your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Next to me is my wife. Well, she took a chance when I met. We have 45 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: And behind you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Behind me is my son. Well, this is my-- the only treasure who I got.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you're going to get married, Chuck? Hm? That's why your dad is here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Uh huh.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's nice. Congratulations.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's it like having a man who is a survivor for a father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I think of him as my father. And I don't think of him in other regards, even though that's his history. But it's kind of interesting that the temple I belong to has-- I've grown in towards a little bit more orthodox, more traditional. And it's right next to the restaurant that Steven Spielberg's mother owns. And Steven Spielberg, who's in charge of this whole history that you're filming today, was honored by my temple Aish Hatorah Jerusalem. And they're located in Los Angeles and many cities all over the world. And the wedding that I'm going to have in a few days is going to be a traditional orthodox wedding. So we've all survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that's what all this is about is to remember that fact.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Julia, when you married, you married a survivor that-- and you were American-born. What-- that must have been a very different experience for you, a very strange experience.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us a little bit about what attracted this foreign-born man, who spoke very little English. What was the attraction to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I found-- I don't know what to say. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] He was different the-- than the man that I had gone out with before. And he was, he was going to build a home [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] [SOBS] and a family. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewee
text: What are you crying? [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: And you wanted to be part of that? Thank you. Thank you all. Thank you. Boris, we're going to look at pictures of your family now.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, tell us about this picture. Yeah. This is my family picture before the war. It was taken in 1925. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] The first row, where I'm sitting, is my hand is under the chin. My name is Boris Abel. On the second one is my youngest bother, Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: He's the one seated on the floor?

speaker: interviewee
text: Seated on the floor.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: The second row on the left side is my oldest brother, Saul. The second one is my mother, Sonya. And the third one is my father, [BACKGROUND NOISE] Schneir Benjamin. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The third row on the left is my brother Ulchik. The second one is my sister Rachel. The third one is my brother Isaac. And the fourth one is my sister Michla.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was the picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: The picture is taken in Panevezys, Lithuania.

speaker: interviewer
text: Good.

speaker: interviewer
text: Rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, who is this? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Boris?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my brother Isaac. He's a survivor from the Germans and from the Russian Communists. And he left, after the war, for Israel. And he died in Israel in-- how many years ago?

speaker: interviewee
text: About four.

speaker: interviewee
text: Five years? In 19--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this picture was probably taken when?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe 10 years ago. I don't--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. OK. Boris, tell us about this picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is by brother Israel. He's a survivor from the Nazi-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] from the Nazis, the German Nazis. And he's a survivor from [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Siberia concentration-- labor camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where is he now?

speaker: interviewee
text: He's now in Israel, in Herzliya.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this picture was taken where and when?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is taken in Latvia.

speaker: interviewer
text: After the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: After the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: So maybe 1940--

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. It's hard to say, because I don't want to say, because sometime we'll say and at this time he was in Siberia. How could they take a picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. So some time shortly after the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: After the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Boris, who's this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my picture, Boris Abel. I took this in 1948 in Munich, Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. OK. So who is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my brother Israel's son. He is born in Riga in 19-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] it's 40 years since when he's born.

speaker: interviewer
text: I think you said 1957.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this picture might have been taken, like, maybe 1962, '63?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is taken 1963.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he's-- what's his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name is Isaac.

speaker: interviewer
text: Abel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Isaac Abel-- Abelski.

speaker: interviewer
text: Abelski. OK. Good.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, there's a picture of me and him together that my mom has.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is Boris Abel with his wife, Julia, made-- taken in 1952.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Taken in Salem, Massachusetts.

speaker: interviewer
text: How come you were so dressed up?

speaker: interviewee
text: We dressed up because we was going to a wedding for my wife's cousin.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, you sure look glamorous. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, we're rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my daughter, Susan Abel. [BACKGROUND NOISE] The picture is taken in Peabody, Massachusetts, in 19-- what's it in?

speaker: interviewee
text: '91, I'd say.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Now you--

speaker: interviewer
text: Roll, and now.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my son Charles, with his bride, Dana Scoff.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was the picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: The picture is taken in Los Angeles, California, in 1995.

speaker: interviewer
text: Good work, OK. [LAUGHTER] It's a wrap.
